extracted_snippet: City Of Oakland To reduce risk and increase resilience equitably, the mission of the City of Oakland Local Hazard Mitigation Plan is to establish and promote a comprehensive mitigation strategy and efforts to protect the whole community and environment from identified natural and human-caused hazards. HAZARD MITIGATION OVERVIEW The City of Oakland (City) has updated its local hazard mitigation plan (LHMP) to continue its commitment to reducing risk of natural hazards for the residents, assets, environment, and community lifelines within the city. The LHMP update forms the foundation of the City’s long-term strategy to reduce disaster losses and break the cycle of disaster damage, rebuilding, and repetitive damage. This plan aligns with federal and state hazard mitigation planning regulations and requirements to ensure the City remains eligible for pre- and post-disaster mitigation funding through the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA).

extracted_snippet: ES-2 PLANNING PROCESS The City of Oakland 2026 Hazard Mitigation Plan is an update of the 2021 Hazard Mitigation Plan. The Oakland Fire Department’s (OFD) Emergency Management Services Division (EMSD) managed the LHMP update and will oversee its implementation and annual updates. The planning area for the Hazard Mitigation Plan was defined as the incorporated area of the City of Oakland. The planning process for this LHMP update, consisted of the following steps:  Organization of Resources –The City organized the Project Management Team (PM), Core Planning Team (CPT), and Steering Committee (SC) (see Table 3-1). These groups met and coordinated throughout the planning process.  Public Engagement and Outreach – A robust public engagement strategy was implemented.  Update Community Capabilities – City departments assessed capabilities related to implementing mitigation initiatives.  Identify Hazards – The Core Planning Team and Steering Committee identified and confirmed hazards to include in the plan.  Risk Assessment – Using best available data and with input from the CPT and SC, the consultant team performed a risk assessment of each natural hazard of concern.  Mitigation Actions and Implementation Strategy – Through a consultant-facilitated process, City departments and stakeholders assembled to develop objectives and mitigation actions.  Assemble and Review the Plan –Black & Veatch (the consultant team) updated and assembled the draft plan with input from the planning groups and City staff. The draft plan was open for public comment for a two-week period.  Plan Approval and Adoption – The LHMP is approved by FEMA and the California Office of Emergency Services (Cal OES). The plan is adopted by City Council and is adopted into the General Plan Safety Element. The City chose to adopt the plan pending Cal OES and FEMA approval to expedite the City Council adoption process. Additionally, the City adopted the LHMP as an appendix to the General Plan’s Safety Element for plan integration. Cal OES performed a review of the draft and FEMA issued approval on Month Day, 2026. The Project Management Team (PM) and Core Planning Team (CPT) facilitated the development of the 2026 LHMP update. BUILDING THE PLANNING TEAMS The City brought together a diverse and inclusive group of individuals to participate, develop, and implement the LHMP. A Project Management (PM) team, a Core Planning Team (CPT) and Steering Committee (SC) were responsible for overseeing, coordinating, and executing the planning process:  Project Management Team - The PM team was comprised of key personnel from EMSD and discipline leads from the consultant team.  Core Planning Team - The CPT was comprised of key personnel from the City and discipline leads from the consultant team.  Hazard Mitigation Steering Committee – Included 23-members comprised of CPT members, other city personnel, and neighboring jurisdiction stakeholders. The PM Team and the CPT facilitated the development of this plan update, consisting of staff from several departments of the City of Oakland and a contract consultant. A 23-member SC of city staff and areawide stakeholders oversaw the plan development. (As many departments had non-voting members and/or alternates, a total of 39 individuals participated in the SC.) Coordination with other local, state, and federal agencies involved in hazard mitigation occurred throughout the seven-step planning process. The CPT and SC reviewed the City’s previous hazard mitigation plan (the 2016-2021 Local Hazard Mitigation Plan), the 2023 California State Hazard Mitigation Plan, and existing programs that may support hazard mitigation actions. A Whole Communities Team included City staff acting as equity subject matter experts, convening as needed to provide recommendations and promote inclusion of and benefit to Equity Priority Communities and individuals with AFN. Members of the Whole Communities Team were also participants in the CPT and SC. RISK ASSESSMENT A risk assessment describes the risks associated with each identified hazard of concern. A general profile of the hazard as it impacts the City was updated and an assessment was performed to determine the effects and the potential damage of the hazard. For this LHMP update, total and Equity Priority Communities are considered in the risk assessment. The CPT and SC reviewed available information to determine what types of hazards may affect the City, how often they can occur, their potential severity, and considered the hazard groupings in the State of California HMP for consistency with the State’s approach to mitigation planning. The following hazards are included in the 2026 LHMP:  Dam Failure  Drought  Earthquake  Flood  Landslide  Sea-Level Rise  Severe Weather  Tsunami/Seiche  Wildfire

extracted_snippet: COMMUNITY CAPABILITIES The LHMP process included a robust assessment of Oakland’s capabilities, based on information provided by City departments. This process was designed to identify any gaps that need to be addressed for successful implementation of the mitigation actions. The following capability types are analyzed in this LHMP:  Plans, Programs, Ordinances and Authorities  Administrative and Technical Capabilities  Fiscal Capabilities  NFIP Capabilities  Adaptive Capacity to Climate Change Capabilities  Public Education and Outreach The capability assessment results indicate that the City is well-equipped to implement mitigation strategies with existing capabilities. MITIGATION STRATEGY The mitigation strategy is the long-term blueprint for reducing the potential losses identified in the risk assessment. It describes how the City will accomplish the overall purpose, goals, and objectives of the planning process. Goals, Objectives, and Actions The SC reviewed and udpated the mitigation goals from the prior plan to reflect the current focus of the City of Oakland, to integrate current citywide plans, and to add an equity focus where feasible. The 2026 LHMP has five goals of equal priority:  Goal 1. Advance equity, reduce racial disparities, and ensure ADA accessibility for people with disabilities and individuals with access and functional needs in all phases of preparing for, responding to, and recovering from natural hazards.  Goal 2. Protect life, property, the environment, and natural and cultural resources equitably.

extracted_snippet: Hazard mitigation reduces the loss of life, property, and environmental damage caused by natural and human-made disasters. Hazard Mitigation Plan consists of long-term and short-term policies, programs, projects, and other activities. The City of Oakland prepared this Local Hazard Mitigation Plan (LHMP) to better protect the residents, property, and assets throughout the City from the effects of natural hazards, and to establish eligibility for pre- and post- disaster FEMA grants. Hazard mitigation plays a crucial role in emergency management by working to reduce the impacts of disasters on individuals, communities, and important assets. By implementing mitigation measures, we can help prevent the same areas from being repeatedly impacted by disasters. Mitigation is part of the emergency management cycle, which is divided into four phases:  Preparedness is when we develop or update activities, programs and systems before an event happens. These activities are often tested (or exercised) in non-emergency situations to determine their effectiveness. Emergency managers also assess potential risks, hazards, and vulnerabilities in this phase.  Response focuses on the immediate and short-term effects of a disaster. It is usually focused on life safety and preventing immediate damage.  Recovery is a long-term phase that looks to return a community to normal, or to a more resilient state, after a disaster.  Mitigation focuses on building (or rebuilding) in ways that reduce the risk more permanently. It is an activity that can occur at any point in the emergency management cycle. For example, communities can undertake mitigation actions before a disaster (the preparedness phase) or while rebuilding after a disaster (the recovery phase) (FEMA 2023).

Section 322 of DMA 2000 emphasizes the need for state and local government entities to closely coordinate on mitigation planning activities and requires a LHMP for any local government applying for federal mitigation grant funds. PLAN UPDATE – WHAT HAS CHANGED Preparation of the 2026 City of Oakland Local Hazard Mitigation Plan continues the hazard mitigation planning process that has been in place in the City of Oakland since the early 1970s, with the adoption of the first Seismic and Safety Elements to the City’s General Plan. The City of Oakland is a leader in the regional discussion of hazards, hazards mitigation, and disaster recovery. The City was designated one of the first Disaster Resistant Communities in the United States, as well as one of the first “100 Resilient Cities.” This is the fourth update to the City of Oakland’s initial 2006 hazard mitigation plan (previously updated in 2011, 2016, and 2021). Prior plan updates reconciled changes or enhancements made to the plan as required by FEMA for local hazard mitigation plan updates. This section reconciles changes and enhancements to the 2021 update. 2.1 THE 2021 PLAN Oakland’s 2021 hazard mitigation plan established a 22-member Steering Committee of City staff and area stakeholders to oversee all phases of the planning process. The mission of the 2021 LHMP was: To equitably reduce risk and increase resilience, the mission of the City of Oakland local hazard mitigation plan is to establish and promote a comprehensive mitigation strategy and efforts to protect the whole community and environment from identified natural and manmade hazards. The following goals and objectives were determined in alignment with the City’s mission statement. 2.1.1 2021 Hazard Mitigation Plan Goals 1. Protect life, property, the environment, and natural and cultural resources. 2. Increase public awareness of and the prevention and preparedness for risks. 3. Coordinate with other programs that can support or enhance hazard mitigation. 4. Increase the effectiveness of emergency services provided to the City. 5. Pursue feasible, cost-effective, and environmentally sound hazard mitigation measures. 6. Increase adaptive capacity to reduce risk from hazard impacts based on a changing climate. 7. Reduce racial disparities in how communities prepare for, respond to, and recover from local hazards. 2.1.2 2021 Hazard Mitigation Plan Objectives 1. Reduce repetitive losses due to flood, fire, and earthquake by informing land use, design, and construction policies. 2. Identify natural and manmade hazards that threaten life and property in the City. 3.

extracted_snippet: 2-3 2.2 WHY UPDATE? 2.2.1 Federal Eligibility In response to the requirements of the DMA 2000, which requires local governmental agencies to develop and update its LHMP every 5 years, this plan serves as the 2026 update to the 2021 Local Hazard Mitigation Plan. The City of Oakland LHMP update is in alignment with FEMA’s Local Mitigation Planning Policy Guide (April 2025), FEMA’s Local Mitigation Planning Handbook (June 2025), and the State of California planning requirements. Title 44 of the Code of Federal Regulations (44 CFR) stipulates that hazard mitigation plans must present a schedule for monitoring, evaluating, and updating the plan (see Section 20.4). This provides an opportunity to reevaluate recommendations, monitor the impacts of actions that have been accomplished, and determine if there is a need to change the focus of mitigation strategies. The Robert T. Stafford Act requires that jurisdictions have current hazard mitigation plans to pursue and receive certain federal grant funding. 2.2.2 Changes in Development Tracking previous and future growth in potential hazard areas provides an overview of increased exposure to hazards within a community. This requirement ensures that the mitigation strategy continues to address the risk and vulnerability of existing and potential development and takes into consideration possible future conditions that could impact vulnerability. As of January 1, 2025, the reported population estimate for the City of Oakland was 426,457, representing a decrease of 1.07% from the 2021 reported population (California Department of Finance 2025). The total number of 191,411 housing units was reported, a 6.23 percent increase, and the average number of persons per household was 2.31, a decrease of 6.97 percent, for the same time frame. extracted_snippet: 3.1 INTRODUCTION The following section describes the overall process of developing the LHMP, including how it was prepared, who was involved, and how stakeholders and the public participated. To adhere to the requirements of the DMA 2000 and ensure that the planning process received wide and robust support from regional and local stakeholders and the public, the approach to the planning process and plan documentation included the following:  The 2026 City of Oakland LHMP is a single jurisdictional plan and considers all natural hazards that pose a threat to the City. This satisfies the natural hazards requirements specified in DMA 2000 and Element B of FEMA’s Local Mitigation Planning Policy Guide (April 2025).  The City selected a contract consultant, Black & Veatch Corporation (the consultant team), to assist with the development and implementation of the plan.  This LHMP was developed following the process outlined by DMA 2000, FEMA’s Local Mitigation Planning Policy Guide (April 2025), and the State of California requirements for mitigation planning. Following these processes ensures that all requirements have been met and supports state and federal reviews of the LHMP. The planning process for this LHMP update, consisted of the following steps: 1. Organization of Resources –City staff defined and organized the Project Management Team (PM), Core Planning Team (CPT), and Steering Committee (SC) (see Table 3-1). These groups met and coordinated throughout the planning process. 2. Public Engagement and Outreach – A robust public engagement strategy was implemented, focused on reaching diverse groups of community members. Local Plan Requirement A1 – 44 CFR Part 201.6(c)(1) The plan shall document the planning process used to develop the plan, including how it was prepared, who was involved in the process, and how the public was involved.

CITY OF OAKLAND PROFILE This chapter provides general information about the City of Oakland, including its historical information, physical setting, general building stock, land use, population, demographics, population trends, and community lifelines. Analyzing this information leads to an understanding of the study area, including economic, structural, and population assets at risk, and of concerns that could be related to hazards analyzed in this plan (e.g., low-lying areas prone to flooding, high percentage of vulnerable persons in an area). 4.1 OVERVIEW 4.1.1 Geographic Overview The City of Oakland is a municipality in Alameda County, California, on the eastern shore of the San Francisco Bay opposite San Francisco. The City has a total area of 78 square miles and is bordered by 19 miles of coastline to the west and hills to the east, with views of both the San Francisco Bay and the Pacific Ocean. Cities adjacent to Oakland include Berkeley to the north; San Leandro to the south; Alameda across the Oakland Estuary; Piedmont, a small city surrounded by Oakland; and Emeryville, a city along the bay between Oakland and Berkeley. Oakland is the only city in the United States with a natural saltwater lake, 115-acre Lake Merritt, wholly contained within its border. 4.1.2 Historical Overview Oakland has a rich and complex history shaped by geography, industry, migration, and infrastructure development. These historical factors have deeply influenced the city’s vulnerability to natural and human-caused hazards. Oakland was founded on unceded land of the Chochenyo-speaking Ohlone indigenous group, the Lisjan, who are the original stewards of this land. After the arrival of Spanish missionaries in the 1760s, many Lisjan people were forced into labor camps at missions and baptized into the Catholic faith. By the late 1840s, many other Ohlone were forced into remote reservations or killed. The Lisjan continue to revitalize their cultural practices and uphold their responsibilities to protect and care for their ancestral homeland. Between the 1770s and 1849, Spanish settlers and later Mexican ranchers established large land grants (ranchos) in the area. Oakland’s incorporation in 1852 coincided with California’s Gold Rush economy. Its proximity to San Francisco and access to deep-water ports made it a hub for shipping and manufacturing. extracted_snippet: 4-2 Oakland’s population surged in the early 20th century, driven by migration from the Midwest and South, as well as waves of immigrants from Asia and Europe. African Americans migrated during the Great Migration, seeking jobs in shipyards and factories. These communities often settled in low-lying, industrial areas where housing was affordable but hazard exposure was high. Urban growth led to dense housing in flatlands, increasing vulnerability to earthquakes and fires; infrastructure strain, as water and sewer systems lagged behind population growth, heightening public health risks during floods or disasters; and transportation expansion, including streetcars and early highways, which improved mobility but also fragmented neighborhoods and created evacuation challenges. World War II brought massive shipbuilding operations to Oakland’s waterfront. Oakland became a defense industry powerhouse, attracting tens of thousands of workers. This boom intensified hazard risks: industrial accidents and fires became more common in shipyards and factories; air and water pollution worsened, creating long-term environmental health hazards; housing shortages led to hastily built structures, often lacking seismic resilience. After the war, the construction of Interstate 880 and other freeways reshaped Oakland. While highways improved regional connectivity, they introduced new vulnerabilities: earthquake risk for elevated freeway structures (tragically realized during the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake when the Cypress Freeway collapsed); flooding and drainage issues due to paved surfaces and altered watershed patterns; and community displacement, concentrating low-income residents in hazard-prone areas. By the 1970s, Oakland faced economic decline as manufacturing jobs disappeared. Abandoned industrial sites became environmental hazards, with contaminated soils and derelict buildings posing fire and health risks. Social vulnerability increased as unemployment rose and public investment lagged. Hazard mitigation during this era focused on earthquake preparedness, spurred by growing awareness of the Hayward Fault’s proximity; flood control projects, including improvements to stormwater systems; and fire prevention, particularly in the wildland-urban interface of the Oakland Hills. extracted_snippet: 4-3 Mitigation measures included strict fire codes for hillside construction; vegetation clearance programs and defensible space requirements; and mutual aid agreements for firefighting resources. Today, Oakland faces a complex hazard landscape shaped by its history: seismic risk remains high due to the Hayward Fault; flooding and sea-level rise threaten low-lying neighborhoods and former wetlands filled for industrial use; wildfire risk persists in the hills, exacerbated by climate change; and social vulnerability continues in historically marginalized communities concentrated in hazard-prone areas. Recent mitigation efforts include green infrastructure projects to manage stormwater and reduce flood risk; climate adaptation planning, focusing on sea-level rise and heat resilience; and community-based preparedness programs, addressing equity in disaster response. Oakland’s history reveals a recurring theme: economic and infrastructural decisions have long-term consequences for hazard vulnerability. Industrial development, transportation networks, and housing patterns created exposure to earthquakes, floods, fires, and environmental hazards. Migration and social inequities compounded these risks, making hazard mitigation not just a technical challenge but a social imperative. Today’s resilience strategies—seismic retrofits, fire prevention, and climate adaptation—are informed by these historical lessons. Oakland’s future depends on integrating hazard mitigation with equitable development, ensuring that all communities can withstand and recover from the hazards shaped by the City’s past. 4.1.3 Government The City of Oakland has a Mayor-Council form of government, where the mayor, one councilmember at-large and seven district based councilmembers are elected to serve four- year terms. Through the Mayor and the Mayor-appointed City Administrator, the City Council establishes policy and directs City departments. The Administrator’s office enforces policies, makes recommendations to the Council, and coordinates projects and initiatives assigned by the Council. The City Council is responsible for adopting this local hazard mitigation plan, while the City Administrator is responsible for overseeing its implementation. The natural region of the Coast Ranges is between the Pacific Ocean and the Great Valley, extending from the Oregon border to the Santa Ynez River near Santa Barbara. Discontinuous northwest-trending mountain ranges, ridges, and intervening valleys characterize this province. Much of the Coast Range province is composed of marine sedimentary and volcanic rocks that form the Franciscan Assemblage. The Franciscan Assemblage in this region represents some of the oldest rocks in the region and consists primarily of greenstone (altered volcanic rocks), basalt, chert (ancient silica-rich ocean deposits), and sandstone that originated as ancient sea floor sediments. The San Francisco Bay is in a broad depression in the Franciscan bedrock resulting from an east-west expansion between the San Andreas and the Hayward fault systems. The source material for these alluvial deposits comes from the Berkeley Hills. Ruptures along the nearby San Andreas Fault caused severe earth movement in the San Francisco Bay Area in 1906 and 1989. San Andreas quakes induce creep (movement occurring on earthquake faults) in the Hayward fault, which runs directly through Oakland, Berkeley, San Jose, and other Bay Area cities. 4.3 SENSITIVE RESOURCES 4.3.1 Historical and Cultural Resources Oakland’s wealth of historic buildings and neighborhoods is matched by few other California cities. These artifacts reflect the City’s rich multicultural history, from earliest times to the present. The materials and workmanship used are impossible or costly to obtain today. In 1994 the City of Oakland adopted a Historic Preservation Element as part of its General Plan. The Historic Preservation Element is based on two broad goals:  Use historic preservation to foster economic vitality and quality of life.  Prevent unnecessary destruction of properties of special historical, cultural, and aesthetic value. The Element spells out these goals through policies and actions that govern how the City treats “Designated Historic Properties” (landmarks, districts, and heritage properties) and “Potential Designated Historic Properties.” Potential Designated Historic Property is the broadest definition of “historic” under the Preservation Element. It is a description, not a designation. It is a category based on Planning Department survey ratings. The ratings report what the survey has found throughout Oakland, on a scale of A (“highest importance”) through E (“of no particular interest”). The City considers any property that has at least a potential rating of C (“secondary importance”) or that could contribute to a potential primary or secondary district to warrant consideration for possible preservation. extracted_snippet: Figure 4-5 State of California and City of Oakland Population Growth by Decade 4.4.2 Equity Priority Communities Equity Priority Communities are populations sharing a particular characteristic, as well as geographic communities, which have been systematically denied a full opportunity to participate in aspects of economic, social, and civic life. Equity Priority Communities represent areas where there is the potential for loss within an individual or social group. The term recognizes that some traits influence an individual’s or group’s resilience. This is their ability to prepare, respond, cope or recover from an event (FEMA 2023). These communities may be more at-risk during hazard events due to a variety of factors, such as their physical and financial capacity to react or respond effectively, as well as the location and quality of their housing. Those with greater vulnerability may experience more severe impacts during emergencies or disasters. It is essential for public officials to consider the distinct needs of vulnerable populations in order to ensure their safety. Note that throughout this LHMP, hazard mapping depicts Equity Priority Communities with an “EJC composite indicator score” based on the General Plan definition described below. Environmental Justice Communities The City of Oakland used the identified Environmental Justice Communities (EJC) from the City’s General Plan Environmental Justice Element to determine the Equity Priority Communities exposed to and at risk from natural hazards. As part of the General Plan Environmental Justice Element, the City developed a screening analysis to identify EJC that consists of a composite set of indicators (quantitative metrics of EJ issues). Each indicator is scored and then combined to identify the topmost cumulatively burdened communities (City of Oakland 2023). It represents the economic and cultural activities (e.g., agricultural, residential, industrial, mining, and recreational uses) that are practiced at a given place. Public and private lands frequently represent very different uses (U.S. EPA 2024). Land cover describes how much of an area is covered by forests, wetlands, impervious surfaces, agriculture, and other land and water types. Water types include wetlands or open water (National Ocean Service 2024). Land use varies from land cover in that certain uses may not always be immediately apparent (e.g., land utilized for timber production without harvest for an extended period and wooded areas designated as wilderness may both appear as forested areas but serve different purposes) (U.S. EPA 2024). The City of Oakland is highly urbanized. Of the total land, over 95 percent is developed. Residential neighborhoods are spread throughout the city. Table 4-6 summarizes the area and Figure 4-7 illustrates the location of current land uses in the City of Oakland. Local Plan Requirement B2 - 44 CFR Part 201.6(c)(2)(ii)(C) The plan should describe vulnerability in terms of providing a general description of land uses and development trends within the community so that mitigation options can be considered in future land use decisions.
